serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of green apple juice is one glass, approximately 300 grams.

nutrient_source.title: FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Adapted from USDA FoodData Central for 'Apple juice, from concentrate, with added ascorbic acid' (FDC ID 170177), as specific green apple juice data is not available. Values adjusted to reflect typical fruit juice composition.

gi_source.title: Apple juice
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value for apple juice (41) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated based on 300g serving size and 11.3g carbohydrate/100g.

hypertension.guidance: Green apple juice, despite low sodium, contains significant sugar (9.6g/100g) and minimal fiber, which can contribute to insulin resistance and indirectly raise blood pressure; consume in moderation or opt for whole fruit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content, rapid glucose absorption, low fiber
type2_diabetes.guidance: Green apple juice has a moderate glycemic index (41) but its high sugar content (9.6g/100g) and lack of fiber can lead to rapid blood glucose spikes; it is best avoided or consumed in very small, controlled portions with a meal containing protein and fiber.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar intake, potential for inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages like green apple juice is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease due to its impact on blood sugar, inflammation, and triglyceride levels; whole fruit is a healthier alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green apple juice is a refreshing beverage made by blending or pressing green apples, often with added water or a touch of sugar to balance its tartness. It typically has a crisp, slightly sour, and sweet flavor profile. Nutritionally, it is primarily composed of carbohydrates, mainly sugars, with very little protein, fat, or dietary fiber compared to whole apples. While it provides some vitamin C and potassium, the juicing process removes much of the beneficial fiber found in the whole fruit. This makes it a high-sugar beverage that can lead to rapid blood glucose increases, posing a concern for individuals with diabetes or those managing blood pressure and cardiovascular health. It is commonly served chilled as a thirst quencher or as part of a meal.
common_user_queries: nutritional value of green apple juice

confidence_reason: Nutrient data adapted from a general apple juice entry in USDA FoodData Central, as specific 'green apple juice' data was not available. GI data is exact for apple juice from University of Sydney. Health suitability based on general fruit juice guidelines.
notes: Original input 'per_100g' values were used as a guide, but replaced with USDA FoodData Central values for a more reliable source. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g were set to -1 in the input but have been populated from the USDA source. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero.
